subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are true ?
A. If two orbitals have the same value of $(n + l)$, the orbital with lower value of $n$ will have lower energy.
B. Energies of the orbitals in the same subshell increase with increase in atomic number.
C. The size of $2p_x$ orbital is less than the size of $3p_x$ orbital.
D. Among $5f$, $6s$, $4d$, $5p$ and $5d$ orbitals, none of the orbitals have $2$ radial nodes.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are true ?
A. If two orbitals have the same value of $(n + l)$, the orbital with lower value of $n$ will have lower energy.
B. Energies of the orbitals in the same subshell increase with increase in atomic number.
C. The size of $2p_x$ orbital is less than the size of $3p_x$ orbital.
D. Among $5f$, $6s$, $4d$, $5p$ and $5d$ orbitals, none of the orbitals have $2$ radial nodes.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Statement A: According to the $(n+l)$ rule, if two orbitals have the same $(n+l)$ value, the orbital with the lower value of $n$ has lower energy. This statement is true.

Statement B: As the atomic number increases, the effective nuclear charge ($Z_{eff}$) increases. The energy of an orbital is proportional to $-\dfrac{Z_{eff}^2}{n^2}$. Thus, the energy becomes more negative, meaning the energy of the orbitals in the same subshell decreases with an increase in atomic number. This statement is false.

Statement C: The size of an orbital increases with an increase in the principal quantum number $n$. Therefore, the size of the $2p_x$ orbital is less than the size of the $3p_x$ orbital. This statement is true.

Statement D: The number of radial nodes is given by the formula $n - l - 1$.
For $5f$: $5 - 3 - 1 = 1$
For $6s$: $6 - 0 - 1 = 5$
For $4d$: $4 - 2 - 1 = 1$
For $5p$: $5 - 1 - 1 = 3$
For $5d$: $5 - 2 - 1 = 2$
Since the $5d$ orbital has exactly $2$ radial nodes, the statement that none of the orbitals have $2$ radial nodes is false.

Therefore, only statements A and C are true.

Answer: A and C only

subject: Chemistry
q: If $\lambda_0$ and $\lambda$ be threshold wavelength and wavelength of incident light, the velocity of photoelectron ejected from the metal surface is:
question: If $\lambda_0$ and $\lambda$ be threshold wavelength and wavelength of incident light, the velocity of photoelectron ejected from the metal surface is:
solution:
The kinetic energy of the ejected electron is given by the equation
$$
\begin{aligned}
h v &=h v_0+\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \quad \because v=\frac{c}{\lambda} \\
& \text { or } \quad \frac{h c}{\lambda} &=\frac{h c}{\lambda_0}+\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\
\frac{1}{2} m v^2 &=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\frac{h c}{\lambda_0} \\
&=h c\left(\frac{\lambda_0-\lambda}{\lambda \lambda_0}\right) \\
\therefore \quad & v^2 &=\frac{2 h c}{m}\left(\frac{\left.\lambda_0-\lambda\right)}{\lambda \lambda_0}\right) \\
& & v &=\sqrt{\frac{2 h c}{m}\left(\frac{\lambda_0-\lambda}{\lambda \lambda_0}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The surface of sodium metal is irradiated with radiation of wavelength $x$ nm. The kinetic energy of ejected electrons is $2.8 \times 10^{-20}$ J. The work function of sodium is $2.3$ eV. The value of $x$ is _____ $\times 10^2$ nm. (Nearest integer)
(Given: $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34}$ J s; $1$ eV $= 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J; $c = 3.0 \times 10^8$ m s$^{-1}$)
question:
The surface of sodium metal is irradiated with radiation of wavelength $x$ nm. The kinetic energy of ejected electrons is $2.8 \times 10^{-20}$ J. The work function of sodium is $2.3$ eV. The value of $x$ is _____ $\times 10^2$ nm. (Nearest integer)
(Given: $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34}$ J s; $1$ eV $= 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J; $c = 3.0 \times 10^8$ m s$^{-1}$)
solution:
The work function of sodium in Joules is:

$\Phi = 2.3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 3.68 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The kinetic energy of the ejected electrons is:

$K = 2.8 \times 10^{-20} = 0.28 \times 10^{-19}$ J

Using the photoelectric equation, the energy of the incident photon is:

$E = \Phi + K$

$E = 3.68 \times 10^{-19} + 0.28 \times 10^{-19} = 3.96 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The wavelength of the incident radiation is calculated using $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E} = \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^8}{3.96 \times 10^{-19}}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{19.8 \times 10^{-26}}{3.96 \times 10^{-19}} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ m

Converting the wavelength to nanometers:

$\lambda = 500 \times 10^{-9}$ m $= 500$ nm

Given that the wavelength is $x$ nm, we get $x = 500$.

Expressing $x$ in the required format:

$x = 5 \times 10^2$

The value to be filled in the blank is $5$.

Answer: $5$

q: If the radius of first orbit of $\mathrm{H}$ atom is $a_0$, the deBroglie wavelength of an electron in the third orbit is
question: If the radius of first orbit of $\mathrm{H}$ atom is $a_0$, the deBroglie wavelength of an electron in the third orbit is
solution:
$r_n=a_0 n^2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& r=a_0 \times(3)^2=9 a_0 \\
& m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} \\
& m v=\frac{n h}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}=\frac{3 h}{2 \pi \times 9 a_0}=\frac{h}{6 \pi a_0} \\
& \lambda=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{h}{h} \times 6 \pi a_0=6 \pi a_0
\end{aligned}
$$

q: Which of the following postulate of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom in not in agreement with quantum mechanical model of an atom ?
question: Which of the following postulate of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom in not in agreement with quantum mechanical model of an atom ?
options: An atom in a stationary state does not emit electromagnetic radiation as long as it stays in the same state
An atom can take only certain distinct energies $\mathrm{E}_1, \mathrm{E}_2, \mathrm{E}_3$, etc. These allowed states of constant energy are called the stationary states of atom
When an electron makes a transition from a higher energy stationary state to a lower energy stationary state, then it emits a photon of light
The electron in a H atom's stationary state moves in a circle around the nucleus
solution: The electron in a H-atom's stationary state moves in a spherical path.

The Lyman series lies in the ultraviolet, in chemistry, the Lyman series is a hydrogen spectral series of transitions and resulting ultraviolet emission lines of the hydrogen atom as an electron goes from n≥2 to n=1  (where n is the principal quantum number), the lowest energy level of the electron.

All of the wavelengths in the Balmer series are in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum (400 nm to 740 nm). The presence of Hydrogen is detected in astronomy by using the H-Alpha line of the Balmer series; it is also a component of the solar spectrum.

All the wavelength of the Paschen series falls in the Infrared region of the electromagnetic spectrum.

Pfund series lie in the infrared.

A – II, B – IV, C – III, D – I

q: Ionisation energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is $19.6 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{atom}^{-1}$. The energy of the first stationary state $(\mathrm{n}=1)$ of $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ is
question: Ionisation energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is $19.6 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{atom}^{-1}$. The energy of the first stationary state $(\mathrm{n}=1)$ of $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ is
solution:
$$
\mathrm{IE}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}=13.6 \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}^2\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right]=13.6 \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}^2 \text { where }\left(\mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}=2\right)
$$
Hence $13.6 \times Z_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}^2=19.6 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{atom}^{-1}$.
$$
\left(\mathrm{E}_1\right)_{\mathrm{L}^{+2}}=-13.6 \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{Uj}^{+2}}^2 \times \frac{1}{1^2}=-13.6 \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}^2 \times\left[\frac{\mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{L}^{+2}}^2}{\mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}^2}\right]=-19.6 \times 10^{-18} \times \frac{9}{4}=-4.41 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~J} / \text { atom }
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Henry Moseley studied characteristic X-ray spectra of elements. The graph which represents his observation correctly is
Given v= Frequency of X-ray emitted
         Z= Atomic number
question:
Henry Moseley studied characteristic X-ray spectra of elements. The graph which represents his observation correctly is
Given v= Frequency of X-ray emitted
         Z= Atomic number
solution:
Moseley's observation on characteristic X-ray spectra of elements relates frequency (v) of X-rays emitted to the atomic number by the following relation:
v=a(Z-b)
The plot of v vs Z is linear.

Based on this observation, the periodic law defined as the physical and chemical properties of the elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of following statement/s which is/are incorrect is ______

A) Line emission spectra are used to study the electronic structure

B) The emission spectra of atoms in the gas phase show a continuous spread of wavelength from red to violet.

C) An absorption spectrum is like the photographic negative of an emission spectrum

D) The element helium was discovered in the sun by spectroscopic method
question:
The number of following statement/s which is/are incorrect is ______

A) Line emission spectra are used to study the electronic structure

B) The emission spectra of atoms in the gas phase show a continuous spread of wavelength from red to violet.

C) An absorption spectrum is like the photographic negative of an emission spectrum

D) The element helium was discovered in the sun by spectroscopic method
solution:
The emission spectrum of a element is the spectrum of frequencies of electromagnetic radiation emitted due to an atom or molecule making a transition from a high energy state to a lower energy state. The energy of the emitted photon is equal to the energy difference between the two states. There are many possible electron transitions for each atom, and each transition has a specific energy difference. This collection of different transitions, leading to different radiated wavelengths, make up an emission spectrum. Each element's emission spectrum is unique.

The emission spectra of atoms in the gaseous phase do not show a continuous spread of wavelength from red to violet, rather they emit light only at specific wavelengths with dark spaces between them.

An absorption spectrum is like the photographic negative of an emission spectrum. The element helium was discovered in the sun by spectroscopic method.

We have to check values of m which lie between -ℓ to+ℓ.

ℓ=2 & ℓ=3 can have m=-2 So possible subshells are 2.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following set of quantum numbers.

  n l m1
A 3 3 -3
B 3 2 -2
C 2 1 +1
D 2 2 +2

The number of correct sets of quantum numbers is
question:
Consider the following set of quantum numbers.

  n l m1
A 3 3 -3
B 3 2 -2
C 2 1 +1
D 2 2 +2

The number of correct sets of quantum numbers is
solution:
For a given 'n' value l will have 0 to n-1 values and for a given 'l' value m will have -m to +m values

For n=3, l=3, is not possible.Similarly for n=2, l=2 is also not possible, as n and l have same values.

So, set A, D are not possible.

If n=3, l can have 0 to 2 values and 'm' can have -2 to +2 values,

So, set B is possible.

If n=2, 'l' can have 0 and 1 values and 'm' can have -1 to +1 values.

So, set C is also possible.

Hence, set B and C are the correct set of quantum nmbers.

question:
For water Δvap H=41 kJ mol-1 at 373 K and 1 bar pressure. Assuming that water vapour is an ideal gas that occupies a much larger volume than liquid water, the internal energy change during evaporation of water is (kJmol-1):
[ Use :R=8.3 J mol-1 K-1
solution:
H2O(l)⇌H2O(g);  ΔHvap. =41 kJ/mol

ΔH=ΔU+ΔngRT

41=ΔU+(1)8.31000×373

ΔU=41-3.0959

ΔU=37.9 kJ/mol ≃ 38 kJ/mol

A. $\mathrm{n}=1, \mathrm{l}=0, \mathrm{~m}_1=0$
B. $\mathrm{n}=2, \mathrm{l}=0, \mathrm{~m}_1=0$
C. $\mathrm{n}=2, \mathrm{l}=1, \mathrm{~m}_1=1$
D. $\mathrm{n}=3, \mathrm{l}=2, \mathrm{~m}_1=1$
E. $\mathrm{n}=3, \mathrm{l}=2, \mathrm{~m}_1=0$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
$\begin{array}{lc} \mathrm{A}: \mathrm{n}=1, \ell=0, \mathrm{~m}_{\ell}=0 & 1 \mathrm{~s} \\ \mathrm{B}: \mathrm{n}=2, \ell=0, \mathrm{~m}_{\ell}=0 & 2 \mathrm{~s} \\ \mathrm{C}: \mathrm{n}=3, \ell=1, \mathrm{~m}_{\ell}=1 & 3 \mathrm{p} \\ \mathrm{D}: \mathrm{n}=3, \ell=2, \mathrm{~m}_{\ell}=1 & 3 \mathrm{~d} \\ \mathrm{E}: \mathrm{n}=3, \ell=2, \mathrm{~m}_{\ell}=0 & 3 \mathrm{~d} \\ \end{array}$
In absence of electric and magnetic fields, all orbitals of 3d are degenerate

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct decreasing order of energy, for the orbitals having, following set of quantum numbers:

(A) n=3,1=0, m=0

(B) n=4,l=0, m=0

(C) n=3,l=1, m=0

(D) n=3,1=2, m=1
question:
The correct decreasing order of energy, for the orbitals having, following set of quantum numbers:

(A) n=3,1=0, m=0

(B) n=4,l=0, m=0

(C) n=3,l=1, m=0

(D) n=3,1=2, m=1
solution:
(A) n+l=3+0=3⇒3s

(B) n+l=4+0=4⇒4s

(C) n+l=3+1=4⇒3p

(D) n+l=3+2=5⇒3d

Higher the n+l value, higher the energy of the orbital and if two orbitals have same n+l value, then the orbital with higher n value has higher energy.

Thus: D>B>C>A.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements

Statement I : According to Bohr’s model of hydrogen atom, the angular momentum of an electron in a given stationary state is quantised.

Statement II : The concept of electron in Bohr’s orbit, violates the Heisenberg uncertainty principle. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements

Statement I : According to Bohr’s model of hydrogen atom, the angular momentum of an electron in a given stationary state is quantised.

Statement II : The concept of electron in Bohr’s orbit, violates the Heisenberg uncertainty principle. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is incorrect but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is correct
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are incorrect

Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is correct but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is incorrect
solution: According to Bohr's postulates the angular momentum in stationary orbit is quantised and is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nh</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is the principal quantum number of the orbit. Bohr's model of the atom assumes fixed orbits and trajectories for the electron. Simultaneously known orbits and trajectories violate the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the graphs shown below does not represent the relationship between incident light and the electron ejected from metal surface?
question: Which of the graphs shown below does not represent the relationship between incident light and the electron ejected from metal surface?
solution:
An electron will be emitted only when the frequency of light is more than the threshold frequency. As the frequency increases, K.E will increase linearly. The graph will be a straight line not passing through origin hence this is not a correct representation. So, this is the correct option.

The number of emitted electrons will depend upon the number of the photons but not on the energy and frequency of the photons.

subject: Chemistry
q: The de-Broglie's wavelength of an electron in the \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit is \(\qquad\) \(\pi \mathrm{a}_0 \cdot\left(\mathrm{a}_0=\right.\) Bohr's radius \()\)
question: The de-Broglie's wavelength of an electron in the \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit is \(\qquad\) \(\pi \mathrm{a}_0 \cdot\left(\mathrm{a}_0=\right.\) Bohr's radius \()\)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 2 \pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{n} \lambda_{\mathrm{d}} \\ & 2 \pi \mathrm{a}_0 \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{Z}}=\mathrm{n} \lambda_{\mathrm{d}} \\ & 2 \pi \mathrm{a}_0 \frac{4^2}{1}=4 \lambda_{\mathrm{d}} \\ & \lambda_{\mathrm{d}}=8 \pi \mathrm{a}_0\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
For hydrogen atom, the orbital/s with lowest energy is/are :
(A) 4 s
(B) $3 \mathrm{p}_x$
(C) $3 \mathrm{~d}_{x^2-y^2}$
(D) $3 \mathrm{~d}_{z^2}$
(E) $4 \mathrm{p}_z$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
For hydrogen atom, the orbital/s with lowest energy is/are :
(A) 4 s
(B) $3 \mathrm{p}_x$
(C) $3 \mathrm{~d}_{x^2-y^2}$
(D) $3 \mathrm{~d}_{z^2}$
(E) $4 \mathrm{p}_z$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
For hydrogen atom and one electron species, the energy of orbitals is decided by the value of principal quantum number. Higher the value of principal quantum number, higher will be the energy of orbital.
$\therefore \quad(B),(C)$ and (D) have orbitals with the lowest energy.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statement : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : 5f electron can participate in bonding to a far greater extent than 4f electrons

Reason R : 5f orbitals are not as buried as 4f orbitals

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statement : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : 5f electron can participate in bonding to a far greater extent than 4f electrons

Reason R : 5f orbitals are not as buried as 4f orbitals

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: A is false but R is true
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution: The distance between the nucleus and 5f orbital is more than the distance between the nucleus and 4f orbital. Hence the hold of nucleus on valence electron decrease in 5f orbital. 5f orbital not buried as 4f oribitals so electron present in 5f orbital experience less nuclear attraction than electron present in 4f orbital. Hence electrons of 5f orbital can take part in bonding to far great extent.

subject: Chemistry
q: The wavelength of an electron and a neutron will become equal when the velocity of the electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> times the velocity of neutron. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is____(the nearest integer)(Mass of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and mass of neutron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>)
question: The wavelength of an electron and a neutron will become equal when the velocity of the electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> times the velocity of neutron. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is____(the nearest integer)(Mass of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and mass of neutron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>)
solution:
λe=λn

hme.ve=hmn.vn

λe=Wavelength of electron

λn=Wave length of neutron

me and Mn=Mass of electron and neutron respectively

ve and vn=Velocity of electron and neutron respectively

h=6.626×10-34Js=Planck's constant

me.x.vn=mn.vn

x=mnme=1.6×10-279.1×10-31

⇒x=169×103

=1.758×103

=1758

subject: Chemistry
q:
The wavelength of electrons accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 40kV is X×10-12 m. The value of x is. (Nearest integer)

Given: Mass of electron =9.1×10-31 kg

Charge on an electron =1.6×10-19C

Planck's constant =6.63×10-34Js
question:
The wavelength of electrons accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 40kV is X×10-12 m. The value of x is. (Nearest integer)

Given: Mass of electron =9.1×10-31 kg

Charge on an electron =1.6×10-19C

Planck's constant =6.63×10-34Js
solution:
De-broglie-wave length of electron:

λe=h2 m(KE)∵e-is accelerated  from rest ⇒KE=q×V

λ=h2mqv

=6.63×10-342×1.6×10-19×9.1×10-31×40×103

=0.614×10-11 m

=6.14×10-12 m

Nearest integer =6

OR

λ=12.3VÅ

=12.3200=6.15×10-12 m

Ans. is 6
